does: This survey provides a comprehensive taxonomy of over 260 papers organized around three fundamental pillars—applications, threats, and defenses—that characterize the emerging landscape of LLM-based agents in cybersecurity. The work maps how agents are deployed in downstream security tasks, identifies inherent vulnerabilities and attack surfaces introduced by agent autonomy, and catalogs countermeasures designed to protect agentic systems. Beyond treating these domains separately, the survey performs pillar-specific and cross-cutting analyses that reveal security-lifecycle coverage of agentic applications, compare red-teaming versus blue-teaming agent paradigms, and illuminate interconnections between capabilities, vulnerabilities, and defenses that have previously remained fragmented across the literature.
connects: This work responds to rapid and uneven growth in research on LLM-based agents applied to cybersecurity tasks, building on foundational literature spanning agent design and autonomous system security, prompt injection vulnerabilities, jailbreaking techniques, and adversarial robustness of large language models. It extends prior surveys on AI security and machine learning safety by focusing specifically on the agent-centric threat model where autonomy and tool use introduce novel attack vectors. The survey synthesizes streams of work on red-teaming and blue-teaming practices developed initially for traditional AI systems and adapts them to the agentic context, while drawing on cybersecurity fundamentals around vulnerability assessment and defense-in-depth strategies applied to AI-native systems.
enables: This unified taxonomy and cross-pillar analysis enables researchers to identify gaps between application capabilities and corresponding defenses, directing future work toward underprotected agent deployment scenarios. By systematizing threats and countermeasures, the survey facilitates the development of principled defense frameworks that account for the full lifecycle of agentic security applications rather than addressing isolated vulnerabilities. It creates a foundation for building composable security primitives for agents and establishes a shared vocabulary for interdisciplinary collaboration between AI safety, cybersecurity, and systems security communities, ultimately supporting the design of trustworthy autonomous agents that can operate safely in high-stakes security contexts while maintaining utility.
